_schema.description: Programmatic SEO data for /alternatives/[competitor]. DORMANT system: every entry ships noindex + sitemap-excluded until the switch-on criteria in C:/Users/Admin/.claude/plans/lexical-snuggling-liskov.md (section 5) are met and the entry is flipped to status:'live' with explicit user go-ahead. scripts/psa-content-gate.mjs hard-fails the build on any thin/unsourced entry.

entryShape.name: Competitor display name
entryShape.dossierVerifiedDate: ISO date the competitor-dossier entry was last verified; gate fails if >60 days old
entryShape.cahAngle: Array of paragraph strings. The unique CAH-differentiated prose. Gate requires >=1200 words combined and this to be the dominant share of page text.
entryShape.verbatimQuotes: Array of { text, name, role, date, source }. Every field required; gate fails on any missing. Verbatim only, copied from docs/seo/competitor-dossier.md.
entryShape.comparisonRows: Array of { dimension, competitor, simplescheduleai }. competitor cell must be neutral product-page framing, never an unsourced feature-absence claim.
entryShape.status: 'draft' (dormant: noindex + excluded) | 'live' (indexable; only after section-5 criteria + user go-ahead)

competitors.name: Homebase
competitors.category: General workforce scheduling platform with a retail, restaurant, and service-industry focus
competitors.positioning: Homebase is a general employee scheduling and time-tracking platform with a free tier for a single location, built for hourly shift coverage across retail, restaurant, and service businesses.
cahAngle: Homebase is a capable general workforce scheduling platform. It carries a 4.4 out of 5 rating on G2 across 271 reviews and a 4.6 out of 5 on Capterra across 1,147 reviews, and it offers a free tier for a single location. Those are strong, well-sampled numbers. For a retail store, a restaurant group, or a service business managing hourly shift coverage, Homebase is a reasonable choice and the ratings reflect that.

For a Critical Access Hospital, the deciding question is not whether Homebase is a good product. It is whether a tool built for hourly retail and restaurant scheduling fits the compliance and staffing reality of a 25-bed hospital. That is a domain-fit question, not a quality question, and it is the honest place to start.

A Critical Access Hospital is a federally designated facility capped at 25 inpatient beds under the CMS Conditions of Participation. Its nurse scheduling is governed by rules a general scheduling app is not built around: the 24/7 registered-nurse coverage requirement, credential verification at the time of each shift, FLSA overtime including the 8-and-80 option common in healthcare, and the documentation a state surveyor expects to see under 42 CFR 485.635. These are not preferences. They are conditions of participation.

Homebase's public product page does not document HIPAA handling, a Business Associate Agreement, CMS 485.635 audit-trail support, FLSA 8-and-80 tracking, or nurse credential constraints. This is not a criticism of the product. A retail and restaurant scheduling tool has no reason to advertise hospital compliance features. It does mean a CAH cannot assume those capabilities are present, and should confirm each one directly with the vendor before relying on the tool for a clinical schedule.

Two verified Capterra reviewers from healthcare settings point at the practical gaps. One is a mobile-scheduling limitation. The other is multi-location tracking and shift-cap behavior. Neither reviewer is talking about a Critical Access Hospital specifically, but both describe the kind of edge a hospital scheduler hits quickly when a general tool is stretched into clinical use.

The mobile-scheduling gap matters at a CAH because the nurse manager is rarely at a desk. At a 25-bed hospital the nurse manager is often on the floor taking clinical shifts. The ability to build and adjust a schedule from a phone is not a convenience there, it is the working condition. A tool where scheduling cannot be done from the app forces the work back to a desktop the manager may not sit at during a shift.

The multi-location and shift-cap limitation matters because hospital staffing is constraint-driven. A unit has a minimum and a maximum safe headcount per shift, credential mixes that must be present, and overtime thresholds that cannot be crossed without cost and compliance consequences. A scheduling tool that cannot cap the number of people on a shift without a paid upgrade is solving a different problem than the one a charge nurse has at 0530 when a nurse calls out.

The deeper issue is the operating model, not any single feature. Homebase is software the organization runs itself. Someone on staff configures it, maintains it, and operates it week to week. At a large multi-location employer that is a reasonable expectation because there is an operations or HR function to own it. At a standalone Critical Access Hospital there is usually no IT department, no dedicated scheduler, and no workforce-management office. The nurse manager is the entire back office and also takes clinical shifts. A tool that assumes an operator assumes a role the hospital cannot staff.

This is the gap a managed service is built to close. SimpleScheduleAI is not a platform the hospital operates. The hospital sends a roster as an Excel file. The service builds and maintains the schedule, applies FLSA and Texas overtime rules including 8-and-80, maintains the CMS 485.635 audit trail, and returns a ranked callout replacement list with credentials confirmed in under two minutes. The nurse manager reviews and approves a finished schedule rather than operating an application. There is no configuration the hospital owns and no integration to maintain.

The contrast is clearest in a callout. With a general scheduling app, a callout at 0530 means someone inside the hospital opens the tool, finds available qualified staff, contacts them in sequence, logs the change, and updates the compliance record. At a retail business a shift lead does this. At a 25-bed hospital the same nurse manager being called by a sick nurse is often the person going on shift at 0700. With the managed-service model, the nurse manager receives a ranked, credential-checked replacement list and the documentation is handled. The difference is measured in clinical time, not clicks.

None of this makes Homebase the wrong tool in general. It makes it the wrong model for a specific situation. Homebase is a strong fit for a business with hourly staff, a person to run scheduling, and no clinical compliance surface. A retail or restaurant operator evaluating Homebase against its actual peers is asking the right question. A 25-bed hospital evaluating Homebase as a nurse scheduler is comparing across categories, and the category gap is the answer.

If a CAH still wants to evaluate Homebase, the due-diligence questions are concrete. Ask the vendor, in writing, whether a Business Associate Agreement is available and whether scheduling data handling is HIPAA-aligned. Ask whether FLSA 8-and-80 overtime can be enforced as a hard rule rather than a report. Ask whether nurse credentials can gate shift assignment and whether the credential state is captured at the time of the shift for a surveyor. Ask whether the schedule, the time and attendance record, and the credential log reconcile into the three documents a 485.635 survey expects. The answers, not the rating, decide fit.

A concrete example shows why the shift-cap limitation is not a minor gap. On a night shift a 25-bed hospital might require exactly one charge-eligible RN, a defined minimum of floor RNs, and no more than a set headcount before the shift is over-budget. A scheduling tool that cannot enforce a maximum on a shift, or cannot gate a slot to a credential, will happily produce a schedule that is technically full but clinically wrong: two nurses where one is funded, or a shift with no charge-eligible RN. A retail floor can absorb a loose headcount. A hospital cannot, because the constraint is patient safety and the labor budget at the same time.

The cost framing follows from that. License price is the wrong anchor for a CAH. A general scheduling app may be inexpensive or free at one location, but the real cost is the nurse manager's time spent operating it and correcting the schedules it cannot constrain. A manager who still spends eight to twelve hours a week building, fixing, and documenting the schedule is carrying a part-time administrative salary in leadership time, regardless of what the tool costs. The question is not the subscription. It is how many of those hours the model gives back.

Track the real number before any tool decision. For one week, log the nurse manager's scheduling hours: schedule building, callout coverage, swap requests, and pre-survey documentation. That total is the number any model has to beat to be worth the change. A general scheduling app that the manager still has to operate does not move that number much. Removing the operating role entirely is what moves it.

The honest summary: Homebase is a well-rated general workforce platform that fits hourly, multi-industry shift scheduling. A Critical Access Hospital is a different problem defined by clinical compliance and a staffing structure with no back office. The right comparison for a CAH is not Homebase versus another app. It is software the hospital must operate versus a managed service that removes the operating burden and carries the compliance documentation as part of the work.

verbatimQuotes.text: I did not like that I could not do scheduling on the app
verbatimQuotes.name: Amber B.

verbatimQuotes.text: Homebase did not allow us to track hours across multiple locations unless we paid extra for it. It was not able to limit number of people on a shift, to our knowledge.
verbatimQuotes.name: Angela P.
